common_name: Pakistan Mulberry (Giant Mulberry, Long Mulberry, Sweet Giant Mulberry)

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after frost danger; Pakistan Mulberry is self-fertile and produces without a pollinator; it thrives in zones 7–10 with warm summers and mild winters, performing particularly well in the Gulf South, California, and similar climates; the extraordinarily long fruit (up to 3 inches) is the defining characteristic that distinguishes Pakistan Mulberry from all other mulberry varieties — no other named mulberry produces fruit even close to this size.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Pakistan Mulberry is drought-tolerant once established; trees bear in year 2–3 from transplant; fruit ripens May–June, producing very long (2–3 inch), deep red to black-purple mulberries with extraordinary sweetness — far more intensely sweet than red or white mulberry; spread a sheet under the canopy and shake branches to harvest ripe fruit. Pakistan Mulberry is a named selection of Morus macroura (the long mulberry of South Asia and Central Asia) introduced and popularized in American home orchards in the late 20th century, valued almost entirely for its exceptional fruit size — the berries of Pakistan Mulberry are dramatically longer than any other mulberry variety, with ripe fruits reaching 2–3 inches in length, nearly twice the length of the longest standard mulberries. This extreme length gives Pakistan Mulberry a visual impact unlike any other berry and a practical advantage in harvesting and fresh eating — the long, cylindrical berries are easy to pick individually and contain more juice and sweetness per fruit than shorter mulberries. The flavor of ripe Pakistan Mulberry is exceptional: intensely sweet, deeply fruity, and far richer than the wild red or white mulberry, with a complex sweetness that improves significantly on the flavor of seedling-grown mulberry trees. The trees are large and vigorous (30–40 feet on standard, smaller with pruning), adapting well to the hot summers of the Gulf South, central California, and the lower Midwest. In zone 9a, Pakistan Mulberry performs very well and has become one of the most-sought specialty fruit trees at Gulf Coast nurseries.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Specialty Fruit Trees
California Rare Fruit Growers — Mulberry Varieties

common_name: Illinois Everbearing Mulberry (Long-Season Red Mulberry Hybrid, Eastern Standard Mulberry)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in spring; Illinois Everbearing is self-fertile and produces without a pollinator; it is a hybrid between white mulberry (Morus alba) and red mulberry (Morus rubra) with the cold-hardiness of the white parent (zones 4–8) and the superior fruit flavor of the red parent; it is the most widely recommended mulberry variety for home orchards in the eastern United States by most university cooperative extension services.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Illinois Everbearing is self-fertile; the trees are fast-growing and begin bearing in year 2–3; fruit ripens over an extended 4–6 week season (June–July in zones 5–7) rather than dropping all at once; the long harvesting period makes it practical to collect ripe fruit repeatedly from the ground or by shaking branches. Illinois Everbearing mulberry is a naturally occurring or deliberately selected hybrid of Morus alba (white mulberry) and Morus rubra (red mulberry) that was selected and propagated in Illinois in the mid-20th century and subsequently became the standard recommended mulberry variety for home orchards in the eastern and midwestern United States. The variety combines the key qualities of both parent species: from Morus rubra, it inherits superior fruit flavor — significantly more complex and berry-like than the often bland white mulberry — along with better fresh-eating quality; from Morus alba, it inherits cold-hardiness to zone 4 and adaptability to a wide range of soils and climates. The most practically important feature reflected in its name is the extended ripening season — berries mature gradually and drop over a period of 4–6 weeks rather than all ripening within 1–2 weeks as many mulberry seedlings do. This extended season makes it much more manageable for home fruit harvesting and wildlife feeding, and reduces the dramatic mess that concentrated mulberry drop can create on driveways, paths, and patios. The fruit quality is good: long (1–1.5 inch), dark red to black-purple berries with sweet, mild, pleasant mulberry flavor — not as intensely sweet as Pakistan Mulberry but far superior to most seedling trees.
sources: University of Illinois Extension — Fruit Tree Recommendations
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Mulberry for Home Orchards

common_name: York Elderberry (Largest-Cluster American Elderberry, New York Station Release)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root shrubs in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; York is a late-ripening American elderberry (Sambucus canadensis) released by the New York State Agricultural Experiment Station, and it is the recommended companion pollenizer for Adams elderberry — plant York alongside Adams (or any early-season American elderberry) for reliable cross-pollination and maximum yield on both plants; York can also be planted as a single specimen but produces better with a companion.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil in full sun; York elderberry is drought-tolerant once established but produces better with consistent moisture; prune back to 6–12 inch stubs in late winter every 2–3 years to rejuvenate; harvest berry clusters in August–September when fully ripe and deep purple-black; process or cook berries within days of harvest as raw elderberries contain cyanogenic compounds and must be cooked for safe consumption. York elderberry was released by the New York State Agricultural Experiment Station at Geneva in the mid-20th century as part of an American elderberry (Sambucus canadensis) improvement program aimed at developing varieties with larger, more consistently sized berries and heavier individual clusters compared to wild-harvested elderberries. York is noted for producing some of the largest individual berry clusters of any named American elderberry variety — the flat-topped cymes (berry clusters) of York regularly reach 10–12 inches across and bear very heavy loads of ripe berries at the dark purple-black ripening stage. The individual berries are also somewhat larger than those of Adams and many other named varieties. This large cluster size and individual berry size make York an excellent choice for home elderberry production where maximum yield per plant and ease of harvest (larger clusters are faster to pick) are priorities. York ripens approximately 2 weeks after Adams, making the York + Adams combination the standard planting recommendation for home elderberry operations wanting to extend the harvest window and ensure reliable cross-pollination between the two varieties. All elderberry processing requires cooking — raw elderberries contain sambunigrin, a cyanogenic glycoside that is destroyed by heat; cooked elderberries are used for syrup, juice, jam, jelly, and elderberry wine.
sources: New York State Agricultural Experiment Station — York Elderberry Release
University of Missouri Extension — Elderberry Production

common_name: Picual Olive (World's Most Planted Oil Olive, Jaén Spain Olive, Stable High-Polyphenol Olive)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root trees in spring in well-drained, low-fertility, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Picual is largely self-fertile and produces good crops as a single tree, though yields improve with cross-pollination from Koroneiki, Frantoio, or another companion; requires approximately 300–400 chill hours; Picual is one of the most cold-tolerant major olive varieties (surviving to approximately 18–20°F when established), making it suitable for zones 8–10.

directions: Plant in well-drained, low-fertility, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Picual is extremely drought-tolerant once established; harvest for oil production in November–December when olives shift from green to purple-black; Picual oil is known for its stability and resistance to oxidation — it keeps significantly longer than most olive oils and maintains its flavor profile without going rancid. Picual olive is the most widely planted olive variety in the world, accounting for approximately 20% of total global olive production — concentrated primarily in the province of Jaén in Andalusia, Spain, which alone produces more olive oil than the entire country of Greece. The variety's global dominance is built on a combination of practical attributes: very high oil content (22–27% by weight, among the highest of any variety), exceptional oil stability and shelf life (Picual oil has very high oleic acid and polyphenol content that makes it resistant to oxidation — a critical commercial advantage since most olive oil goes rancid within 1–2 years), strong cold-hardiness relative to other major varieties, and vigorous, productive trees adaptable to the challenging calcareous, poorly-drained soils of the Spanish meseta. Picual oil has a distinctive flavor profile: robust, fruity, slightly bitter and peppery in the fresh-pressed (unfiltered) state — characteristics that reflect its high polyphenol content (polyphenols provide both the peppery sensation and the health benefits of olive oil). For home oil production, Picual's high oil content and excellent stability make it the most efficient variety for pressing, and its robust flavor is well-suited to the strong tastes associated with premium extra-virgin olive oil.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Olive Varieties
International Olive Council — Picual Variety Documentation
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Olive Production

common_name: Zutano Avocado (Low-Chill Type B Avocado, Early Season Pale Green Avocado)

planting.note: Plant container-grown grafted trees in spring after all frost danger; Zutano is a Type B avocado (flowers open female in the afternoon, shed pollen the following morning) and cross-pollinates effectively with Type A varieties including Hass and Mexicola; Zutano has an early ripening season (September–November), making it valuable for extending fresh avocado harvest before the main Hass season; requires approximately 300 chill hours, among the lowest requirements of any commercial avocado variety.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Zutano performs best with a Type A companion (Hass, Mexicola) for cross-pollination; harvest September through November when fruits are full-sized and soften slightly over 5–7 days after picking; Zutano fruits stay green at ripeness and have a watery, mild flavor — better for cooking than fresh eating but valuable for early-season harvest timing. Zutano avocado is a California-developed variety from the Mexican race lineage with one of the lowest chill requirements of any commercial avocado, performing well in the warmest avocado-growing regions and ripening its crop earlier in the season (September–November) than the dominant Hass variety (January–June harvest). The variety was developed by R.J. Hazzard in Fallbrook, California in the 1920s and entered the California nursery trade where it became valued primarily as a cross-pollinator for Hass and other Type A varieties due to its abundant, reliable Type B pollen production and good overlap with Hass bloom times. Zutano's fruit quality is frankly mild and somewhat watery compared to Hass, Fuerte, or Reed — the flesh is pale yellow-green, less rich in oil, and more watery in texture — making it less valued for fresh eating than the richer varieties. However, Zutano provides practical value as: (1) the best early-season fresh avocado available from your own tree in September when Hass season hasn't started; (2) a reliable Type B pollinator that ensures good fruit set on Hass and other Type A trees in the same garden; and (3) a lower-chill, cold-somewhat-tolerant variety for zone 9 margins where Hass reliability is borderline.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Avocado Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Avocado Production
